coredata.dc:title: Silver-modified nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots as a sensor for formaldehyde in milk using headspace micro-extraction on a single-drop of aqueous nanoparticles dispersion

coredata.dc:description:
Background Fraudulent practices used to distort the quality of milk and derivatives include the addition of formaldehyde.
Results A formaldehyde sensor was developed based on the luminescence of newly proposed N-doped graphene quantum dots modified with silver (N-GQDs-Ag) that were prepared using a simple method. A microdroplet of the nanoparticle dispersion was used to collect formaldehyde vapor by headspace single-drop micro-extraction (HS-SDME). After, the microdroplet was diluted in water, the nanoparticle photoluminescence quenching, caused by the analyte, was measured. The strong luminescent quenching allowed a detection limit at 1.7 × 10−4% w/v. Response was selective towards formaldehyde.
Significance The method was effective and a cost-effective method for screening analysis of milk samples with matrix interferences minimized due to the nature of nanoparticle (prepared using Tollen's reagent) and due to the probing at the headspace of the sample cell. Results were statistically similar to those obtained using liquid chromatography.

head.ce:title: Silver-modified nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots as a sensor for formaldehyde in milk using headspace micro-extraction on a single-drop of aqueous nanoparticles dispersion

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fraudulent practices used to distort the quality of milk and derivatives include the addition of formaldehyde.

A formaldehyde sensor was developed based on the luminescence of newly proposed N-doped graphene quantum dots modified with silver (N-GQDs-Ag) that were prepared using a simple method. A microdroplet of the nanoparticle dispersion was used to collect formaldehyde vapor by headspace single-drop micro-extraction (HS-SDME). After, the microdroplet was diluted in water, the nanoparticle photoluminescence quenching, caused by the analyte, was measured. The strong luminescent quenching allowed a detection limit at 1.7 × 10−4% w/v. Response was selective towards formaldehyde.

The method was effective and a cost-effective method for screening analysis of milk samples with matrix interferences minimized due to the nature of nanoparticle (prepared using Tollen's reagent) and due to the probing at the headspace of the sample cell. Results were statistically similar to those obtained using liquid chromatography.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Formaldehyde is a naturally occurring aldehyde that, at room-temperature, is a colorless and flammable gas with a suffocating odor, being easily soluble in polar solvents, in which formalin (36.5–38% w/v in H2O) is its most known solution. Besides its extensive use in various products manufacture, it is also employed in pharmaceutical and cosmetic industries due to its preservative and antibacterial properties [1] and as a conservative in some foodstuffs [2]. Acute human exposure to formaldehyde can cause allergies, asthma, neurological damage and even cancer [1,3–5].

Dairy products are highly susceptible to bacteria and mold, and pasteurization, or similar treatments at high temperatures, are effectively and safely used to prevent their deterioration. Fraudulent practices employed to distort the quality of milk and derivatives include the addition of formaldehyde, as it improves appearance and smell, especially when dairy products are not handled hygienically [6,7]. As this is of concern, strict quality control of dairy products, especially milk, is required.

Various methods are reported for determining formaldehyde in dairy products, but they usually involve some sample treatment. High-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) is the most commonly used technique despite labor-intensity and time-consumption, as they normally require extraction of analyte and chemical derivatization [8–11]. Absorption and fluorescence spectrophotometry have also been used to determine formaldehyde in milk, but they often require the use of toxic and expensive chromophores to enable near UV–visible range detection [6,12,13]. Electro-analyses [14,15] are usually plagued by interferences due to the presence of other species from complex sample matrices, even after treatment. Other methods based on infrared spectrometry [16–19], time domain nuclear magnetic resonance [20] and electrical impedance [21] have also been reported.

When a practical method is developed, in addition to appropriate figures of merit and adequate metrological features, the process should rely on minimum sample preparation, producing low quantities of toxic waste. In this sense, optical sensors for the detection of formaldehyde have received wider attention. Luminescence-based ones are easy to operate and sensitive [22–25].

Nanomaterials, especially carbon-based quantum dots (CQDs), have been used for sensing formaldehyde [26–30]. They are used as analytical probes in very low quantities due to their optical stability and intense luminescence. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are a type of CQDs that present themselves as flakes of graphene that have suitable bandgaps for emission in the visible part of the spectrum [28], in some cases extending to the near-infrared [29]. The π → π* transition of C=C bonds gives GQDs a strong absorption in the UV, extending to the visible range [28]. GQDs exhibit strong photoluminescence in the visible region with, in many cases, no clear dependency of the emission spectral position upon the nanomaterial size. The general optical behavior of GQDs can be characterized by electron-hole recombination, by the contributions of the quantum confinement effect, doping elements, surface defects and functional groups at their edges [26,30–33]. The incorporation of heteroatoms in GQDs has been an effective way to improve their optical properties [26,27].

When used for analytical sensing, GQDs interact with the target resulting in either luminescence suppression or amplification, sometimes along with emission spectral shift. For instance, GQDs doped with nitrogen (N-GQDs) from ammonia and melamine were used as a sensor for formaldehyde in aqueous solutions (within 9.0–900 μg L−1) as measured photoluminescence from the aqueous dispersion of N-GQDs-melamine decreased when analyte concentration increased [34]. Formaldehyde in an aqueous solution has been probed (LOD of 4.5 μg L−1) by N-GQDs produced by mixing citric acid and urea through a hydrothermal route, then encapsulated using organic species, and as it interacts with formaldehyde, photoluminescence quenching was observed [35]. Detection of formaldehyde in aqueous solutions and food extracts was made using GQDs, obtained by melting citric acid and then dispersing it into NaOH solution. Photoluminescence quenching correlated linearly between 0.05 and 1 μg mL−1 of the analyte [36]. Even though sensitive towards formaldehyde, these probes are still limited to be used for simple aqueous samples, in most cases requiring long periods to allow effective interaction with the analyte.

In the case of volatile analytes such as formaldehyde, headspace single-drop micro-extraction (HS-SDME) allows sampling, extraction and analyte concentration on a single micro-drop of an extracting agent exposed to the headspace above the sample. Interferences imposed by the sample matrix's non-volatile or less volatile components are minimized since the analyte is volatilized and diffused into the extracting solvent that composes the hanging drop. Taking advantage of formaldehyde volatility and considering the advantages provided by the HS-SDME technique and GQDs as an analytical probe, the analytical sensing of formaldehyde can be achieved simply. The newly proposed N-GQDs modified with silver (N-GQD-Ag), produced in an aqueous dispersion by a hydrothermal route (using citric acid, urea and Tollen's reagent) was used as the extractive micro-droplet and photoluminescent probe. Tollen's reagent, composed of [Ag(NH3)2]+, is reactive towards aldehydes leading to the formation of Ag0 [37]. Aiming for high selectivity and virtually no sample preparation, the nanomaterial aqueous dispersion drop was hung onto the headspace above the milk sample, allowing formaldehyde vapor to diffuse and interact with N-GQD-Ag. Then, the drop was transferred to a quartz cuvette for photoluminescence measurements and formaldehyde quantification.

Photoluminescence (steady state and spectrum scanning) was acquired on a luminescence spectrophotometer model LS 55 (PerkinElmer, UK) using 1 cm optical path length quartz cuvettes and 10 nm excitation and emission spectral band passes. When temperature stabilization was required, a thermostatic system with stirring (PTP-1 Fluorescence Peltier System and PCB1500 Water Peltier System, PerkinElmer). A pHmeter (model mPA 210, Tecnopon, Brazil) with a glass membrane electrode, conjugated with a reference Ag/AgCl(KCl sat) electrode, was used. Absorption spectra (UV–visible range) were acquired on a double beam spectrophotometer model Cary 100 (Varian, USA) using 600 nm min−1 scan rate, 10 nm spectral band pass and 1 cm optical path length quartz cuvettes. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra (4000–400 cm−1 range and accumulation of 10 scans) were obtained on Spectrum Two (PerkinElmer), a spectrophotometer model coupled with a universal attenuated total reflectance accessory. Nanoparticle images were made on a field emission scanning electron microscope model JSM-7800F (JEOL, Japan) operating in the scanning transmission electron microscopy (STEM) mode at 30 kV. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) and ζ-potential measurements were made on an SZ-100 Nanopartica series (Horiba, Japan), equipped with a 10 mW laser at 532 nm. Acrylic electrochemical cells containing flat carbon electrodes (6 mm thickness) and polystyrene cuvettes with four optically clear sides were used respectively for ζ-potential and DLS measurements. Total carbon data were acquired on a carbon analyzer model TOC-VCPN (Shimadzu, Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was made under ultrahigh vacuum (∼10−9 mbar) using a surface analysis chamber, equipped with a VG Thermo Alpha 110 hemispherical analyzer, and using a non-monocromatized Al-Kα excitation line. Raman spectra were acquired on a Raman microscope model XploRa (Horiba, Japan) using an excitation wavelength of 532 nm. Energy dispersive X-ray fluorescence spectrometry (EDX-RF) was performed on an EDX 800HS spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan) with Rh tube (excitation source) and Si(Li) semiconductor detector, using semi-quantitative mode. Inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectroscopy (ICP OES) analyses were made on an Optima 7300 DV (PerkinElmer) spectrometer operating at 1400 W, using an Ar plasma flow rate of 15 L min−1, Ar nebulizer flow rate at 0.6 L min−1, Ar auxiliary flow rate at 0.5 L min−1, and the sample aspiration flow rate of 1.5 mL min−1. The emission line employed was Ag (328.068 nm). Chromatographic analyses were performed on a high-performance liquid chromatographic system (1200 series, Agilent, USA) equipped with a G1315C model diode array absorption photometric detector. A heating plate (IKA, Germany), with magnetic stirring capability, was used for temperature and homogenization control in performing the extraction. For lyophilization, a freeze dryer model Micro Modulyo (Edwards, England), coupled with a pump model W22 (WEG, Brazil), was employed.

Ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ cm) was obtained from the Milli-Q gradient A10 ultra-purifier (Millipore, USA). Reagents were of analytical grade or better. Citric acid, urea, sodium citrate, silver nitrate, copper sulfate (pentahydrate), boric acid, sodium hydroxide, acetic acid, acetonitrile (HPLC grade), 2,4-dinitrophenylhydrazine (DNPH) and chromotropic acid were purchased from Merck (Germany). Formalin (36.5–38.0% w/v), nitric acid, sulfuric acid, phosphoric acid and ammonium hydroxide were from Vetec (Brazil). Milk samples were purchased in local supermarkets in the city of Rio de Janeiro, Brazil. Filters and syringe filters (45 μm) were from (Sigma Aldrich, USA). A 25 μL manual chromatography syringe (Hamilton, USA) was used for headspace single-drop micro-extraction (HS-SDME). The chromatographic column was a model Eclipse XDB-C18 (250 × 4.6 mm length, 5 μm average particle size) from Agilent (USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the silver or copper modified nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots by direct hydro-exfoliation of the molten precursor
ce:section.ce:para: Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) were prepared using urea as a source of carbon and nitrogen and using either silver or copper as modifiers, expecting to obtain effective responses towards aldehyde carbonyl groups. A mixture of citric acid (0.5 g or 2.4 mmol) and urea (0.15 g or 2.5 mmol) was heated, in a beaker, to about 270 °C using a heating plate. When the molten mixture reached a yellowish-brown color (about 2 min of heating), the content was added either to 10 mL of a Tollen's reagent solution (1 × 10−3 mol L−1) or to 10 mL of Benedict's reagent solution (1 × 10−2 mol L−1) and kept under magnetic stirring for 20 min. The resulting stock dispersions were stored in amber glass flasks and kept in the dark at room-temperature. These stock dispersions were used to prepare the modified N-GQDs working dispersions by dilution in ultrapure water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the silver-modified nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs-Ag) by refluxing
ce:section.ce:para: The N-GQDs-Ag were produced through a hydrothermal refluxing route based on a literature procedure [35] with modifications. The citric acid (0.84 g or 4 mmol), urea (0.48 g or 8 mmol) and 200 μL of the Tollen's reagent (1 × 10−1 mol L−1) were mixed in 20 mL of water in a round-bottom flask that was adjusted in a condensation system. The flask was placed into a heating mantle and heated at 180 °C for 8 h. The resultant aqueous dispersion (stock N-GQDs-Ag dispersion) was let to cool down to room-temperature, stored in the dark, and used to prepare the N-GQDs-Ag working dispersions by dilution with ultrapure water. N-GQDs-Ag were also prepared with different concentrations of Tollen's reagent and different urea:citric acid proportions (1:1, 1:3 and 1:4 in mmol).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of standard solutions, sample solutions and working solutions
ce:section.ce:para: Tollen's reagent stock solution (1 × 10−1 mol L−1) was prepared by dissolving an appropriate amount of silver nitrate in ammonia solution (pH 9.0). Bennedict's reagent stock solution was prepared by dissolving proper amounts of sodium citrate and copper sulfate pentahydrate in a sodium carbonate solution. Stock nanoparticle dispersions were, in general, previously diluted with water before characterizations and also when using them as analytical probes. Formaldehyde diluted solutions were prepared by diluting (when needed sequential dilutions) appropriate amounts of commercial formaldehyde 36.5–38.0% w/v with water. Before dilutions, actual formaldehyde concentration was determined by titration of a solution containing 4.0 g of the commercial formalin diluted in Na2SO3 (1.00 mol L−1) to 100.00 mL. Titrations (n = 3) were made using H2SO4 (1.00 mol L−1) and thymolphthalein as the indicator.

Milk samples (previously diluted when necessary) were fortified with formaldehyde to simulate adulterations to reach a final concentration from 1.0 × 10−4% w/v to 1.0 × 10−2% w/v. Milk samples were not submitted to any specific treatment but dilution with water: 2-fold dilution (whole milk and 0% lactose whole milk) and 1-fold dilution (semi-skimmed milk). Skimmed milk was not diluted for the analysis.

Different techniques were applied to characterize the N-GQDs-Ag dispersion (citric acid:urea at 1:2 mmol proportion). When required, lyophilization of the centrifuged pellet of the nanomaterial was made in a freeze dryer system where the sample was let to dry overnight at room-temperature, at 1.0 × 10−2 torr. DLS analyses were made after 24 h and 120 h of preparation of nanoparticles. For ζ-potential analysis, dispersions had their pH adjusted (within 3.0 and 10.0) with Britton-Robinson buffer (0.04 mol L−1). 10.0 mL of diluted nanoparticle dispersions (1000 dilution factor with water) were used for total carbon. STEM analysis was made by placing a few microliters of the stock N-GQDs-Ag dispersion onto carbon grids and letting them dry, at room-temperature, before measurements. For Raman analysis, a few microliters of the stock N-GQDs-Ag dispersion were deposited on a silicon substrate and allowed to dry at room-temperature. Then, the 532 nm laser beam of the Raman microscope was used to probe the sample. For EDX-RF analysis, the samples of N-GQDs-Ag were precipitated by centrifugation before washing with water and centrifuging again before placing them onto a solid substrate (W41 filter paper with 10 mm of diameter) using sequential additions of 10 μL (up to 120 μL) before adjusting the substrate in a polyethylene cell. For ICP-OES analyses, the precipitated N-GQDs-Ag was washed with water and, after centrifugation, re-dispersed in water to be submitted to an acidic decomposition before measurements. Samples of N-GQDs-Ag used for FT-IR (using the ATR accessory) and XPS analyses were obtained by water evaporation the dispersion at 60 °C to obtain a gel-like material. Photoluminescence and absorption spectrophotometric measurements were made using water-diluted N-GQDs-Ag dispersions (up to 1000 dilution factor).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of milk using headspace single drop micro-extraction (HS-SDME)
ce:section.ce:para: HS-SDME was performed using a 25 μL manual chromatography syringe and a heating plate, with magnetic stirring capability, for temperature control and homogenization. The procedure was based on a report involving the analysis of metalloids as hydride vapor [38,39]. Briefly, 1.00 mL of the aqueous formaldehyde solutions or milk samples fortified with formaldehyde (both with final concentration from 1.0 × 10−4% w/v to 1.0 × 10−1% w/v) was placed in a 5.0 mL vial that was then sealed with a rubber septum. The vial was placed on a heating plate at 25 °C (monitored with a thermocouple temperature meter) and kept under magnetic stirring. The needle of the microsyringe, containing 5 μL of the concentrated aqueous dispersion of N-GQDs-Ag, was inserted through the rubber septum and adjusted right above the solution's surface. A drop of the N-GQDs-Ag dispersion was created at the tip of the syringe using the entire content in it (5 μL of the dispersion) and exposed to the flask headspace for 5 min while samples were under gentle stirring. Then, the drop was retracted back into the microsyringe to be further transferred to a quartz cuvette containing 3.0 mL of water to measure photoluminescence after 30 s of stirring. A blank comprised of N-GQDs-Ag dispersion was made without exposing it to the sample head-space. A scheme of the procedure is illustrated in Fig. S1.

Steady-state intensity measurements (emission spectrum peak height) were made at 349/441 nm from diluted N-GQDs-Ag dispersions that interacted with formaldehyde (I), then normalizing by the respective blank signal (the diluted N-GQDs-Ag dispersion without contact with formaldehyde or I
0 ) to establish a directly proportional relationship with the concentration of formaldehyde, with K
s (quenching constant) as sensitivity (Eq. (1)).
(Eq. 1)
I
0 /I = 1 + K
s [formaldehyde]

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chromatographic method for the determination of formaldehyde in milk
ce:section.ce:para: The chromatographic method for formaldehyde determination in milk was based on the one reported by Rezende et al. [8]. Whole milk samples were fortified (1.0 × 10−4% w/v, 1.0 × 10−3% w/v, 1.0 × 10−2% w/v and 1.0 × 10−1% w/v) with formaldehyde. Aliquots of 2.5 mL of the milk sample were placed in centrifuge tubes where the sulfuric acid solution (0.1 mol L−1) was added to adjust the pH to about 4. Then, 5.0 mL of 0.1 g L−1 DNPH solution (in acetonitrile) were added to the tubes, which were then vortex-mixed (1 min) and centrifuged (20 min) at 1000 RFC. After cooling overnight, at −4 °C, the aqueous phase was frozen while the organic phase, which remained liquid, was sampled to 5.00 mL volumetric flasks with the final volume adjusted with acetonitrile. These solutions were filtered (0.45 μm syringe filters) into HPLC vial flasks. Formaldehyde-DNPH analytical curve was made with formaldehyde solutions (from 1.0 × 10−4 to 1.0 × 10−2% w/v) prepared in 0.1 g L−1 DNPH solution in acetonitrile. The mobile phase consisted of acetonitrile (A) and ultrapure water (B) (filtered using 0.45 μm membrane and degassed in the ultrasonic bath) with gradient elution starting with 65% v/v of (A), for 6 min, with a linear increase up to 90% v/v until 8 min. Flow-rate was 1.0 mL min−1 with the column kept at 30 °C. The sample injection volume was 10 μL, and detection was made at 360 nm. The retention time for the derivatized product was 4.6 min, and measurements (n = 3) were based on peak area.

The N-GQDs prepared by the direct hydro-exfoliation of the molten precursors either in Tollen's reagent solution (Ag modification) or in Benedict's reagent solution (Cu modification) had their photoluminescence intensities (as aqueous dispersion) monitored over time, and they were found to be reasonably stable for only a few days when their photoluminescence significantly decreases. In addition, the effect arising from interaction with formaldehyde added as a solution produced photoluminescence quenching only at high analyte concentrations (above 1.5 × 10−1% w/v). The N-GQDs modified with silver were relatively more sensitive toward formaldehyde than the ones modified with copper. Therefore, further studies were made with N-GQDs modified with Ag (at the concentration of 1.0 × 10−3 g L−1) prepared by refluxing the mixture of precursors (citric acid:urea ratio of 1:2) in Tollen's reagent solution. At these concentrations of precursors, the photoluminescence was found to be very stable, intense and significantly more sensitive toward formaldehyde and because of this, the reported results are related only to these nanoparticles.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological and structural characterization of N-GQDs-Ag obtained by refluxing
ce:section.ce:para: The fresh N-GQDs-Ag dispersion prepared by refluxing is slightly yellow transparent, turning into a slightly lilac transparent color (Fig. S2A) after 24 h. When the dispersion was stored and protected from ambient light, the color gradually changed to stronger lilac in less than two weeks (Fig. S2B) and then purple within 15–30 days (Fig. S2C). After approximately 90 days, the dispersion turned brown with a dark particle suspension (Fig. S2D), sometimes with the formation of a silver film on the flask wall. Dispersions stored in amber glass flasks, under ambient light, had their color changed to slightly brown after around 15 days, and a darker brown after about 30 days, and after 60, a visible particle suspension was observed. Under UV radiation, the recently prepared dispersion showed a visible blue color luminescence (Fig. S2E) which, according to Li et al. (when preparing N-GQDs under autoclave conditions [35]), is related to the oxidation of GQDs as it is exposed to atmospheric O2. Total carbon (TC) content in the original N-GQDs-Ag dispersion (stock dispersion) was 20.9 g L−1. From this dispersion, the working dispersions (after 1000 times dilution) were prepared with TC of 21 mg L−1.

Dynamic light scattering (DLS) studies showed nanoparticle aggregates associated with color changes. DLS and ζ-potential measurements were performed to obtain hydrodynamic size distributions and the typical surface charge of N-GQDs-Ag dispersed in water. Measurements of ζ-potentials were made, in the function of the pH, after 24 h of production of the N-GQDs-Ag, before and after exposing them to UV (15 min) with measurements made (n = 7) at 25 °C and at angles of 90o or 173°. The use of urea, as one of the precursors, might lead to the incorporation of nitrogen atoms into the graphitic matrix of GQDs in the form of pyridinic-N, graphitic-N and pyrrolic-N, which can contribute with electrons to the π-electron system [40]. The surface of the N-GQDs-Ag, at pH 3.0, is positively charged (Fig. 1 A), probably due to the protonation of N (pyridinic-N and pyrrolic-N acceptors of protons [35]). At pH 4.0 and 5.0, ζ-potential values of about 0.1 and −0.4 mV, respectively, were obtained because of the more limited availability of H+ ions. Negative surface charges of the N-GQDs-Ag are probably due to carboxylic acid and hydroxyl groups, which tend to be deprotonated depending on their characteristic pKa value. As the pH increased, ζ-potential values close to zero were obtained as the material reached the isoelectric point. The N-GQDs-Ag remained stable in an alkaline environment, which can be attributed to the rich nitrogen-containing organic groups on the surface.

When the N-GQDs-Ag were exposed to UV, a ζ-potential value close to zero or slightly negative was obtained at all pH values (Fig. 1B), probably due to the increasing carbonyl groups originating from photochemical oxidation [41]. At pH 3, the measured hydrodynamic size evidenced low nanomaterial aggregation. At lower pH, the smaller size of N-GQDs-Ag (after 24 h of production) was obtained with size increasing as pH value was elevated (Fig. 2 A). From pH 3 to 6, nanoparticle sizes increased from 2.5 nm to 4.6 nm, and as the pH reached the range from 7 to 10, it is observed sizes from 7.5 to 9.9 nm. When the N-GQDs-Ag were exposed to UV, larger and more dispersed sizes were observed (Fig. 2A), as UV tends to promote nanomaterial aggregation [42]. The size of the nanomaterial after 120 h of production (without exposure to UV) was also measured (Fig. 2A), and a small increase in size (in respect to the size measured after 24 h of production) was observed mainly at a pH greater than 7. The sizes of N-GQDs-Ag after 120 h were close to the ones exposed to UV (after 24 h of production). Such an increase in the nanomaterial size can be justified due to the incorporation of functional groups as a consequence of an oxidation reaction from atmospheric O2, which contributes to the aggregation of the N-GQDs-Ag, as suggested by Li et al. [35]. Scanning transmission electron microscopy image (Fig. 2B) corroborates DLS results, with the size distribution of the produced N-GQDs-Ag within 4–20 nm (11.2 ± 2.7 nm average diameter), increasing to about 40 nm as aggregation occurred due to pH increasing or by the incidence of UV.

XPS analyses suggest nitrogen functionalization (organic in nature) of GQDs. The general survey showed characteristic peaks (Fig. 3 A) for C, O and N at binding energies of 285 eV (C 1s), 533 eV (O 1s) and 401 eV (N 1s). The spectrum showed C 1s peaks at 284.9 eV, 285.9 eV, 286.7 eV, 288.2 eV and 289.2 eV (Fig. 3B), assigned to the contributions of C=C and C–C, C–N, C–O–C and C–OH, C=O and COOH, respectively. The deconvoluted spectra (Fig. 3C) also showed peaks at 400.3 eV, 401.2 eV and 402.7 eV, which correspond to pyrrolic-N, graphitic-N, and pyridinic-O, respectively [43]. The peak at 398.8 eV, which corresponds to pyridinic-N, is very small (the area could not be calculated). In contrast, N 1s main peak corresponds to the graphitic bound to N at a binding energy of 401.2 eV, with the area corresponding to 41.19% of the total, while the pyrrolic bound to N and the pyridinic bound to O corresponds to 29.02% and 29.79% respectively.

The vibrational spectrum obtained from the partially dried sample of N-GQDs-Ag (Fig. S3A) indicated the presence of oxygen and amino functional groups on the N-GQDs-Ag. The wide band at 2650-3750 cm−1 refers to O–H and N–H stretching vibrations. Also, bands in the 1030-1630 cm−1 range suggest the presence of stretching vibrations of C=C, C=N, COO-, COOH, C–OH, C–N, C–OH/C–C/−OH and C–H. In addition, amide-carbonyl stretching vibration (−NH–C=O) was observed at 1652 cm−1, indicating the formation of amide groups from carboxylic groups [44–46].

Raman spectrum showed two characteristic peaks of sp2 carbon in graphitic structure (Fig. S3B). The G band was centered at 1570 cm−1 (typical of the E2g phonon of sp2 carbon), and the D band was observed at 1363 cm−1. The presented spectrum is normally found in systems with great disorder extent.

Through XPS and FT-IR analyses, covalent bonding between Ag and C or N could not be identified, which indicates a possible ionic interaction with Ag decorating the surface/edges of GQDs. The presence of Ag in the N-GQDs-Ag (re-dispersed before and after washing with water) was confirmed by EDX-RF (Fig. S4). Through ICP-OES, it was possible to determine that the Ag typical content in the N-GQDs-Ag (after precipitation, washing with water and dissolution in acid) was 430 μg g−1. It is important to mention that acid decomposition was necessary for the ICP OES analyses, indicating that Ag was encapsulated or somehow incorporated in the nanomaterial.

Optical properties of N-GQDs-Ag were studied using up to 1000 times the dilution factor of the stock N-GQDs-Ag dispersion. The extinction spectrum (Fig. S3C) showed a peak of about 200 nm, usually related to the π-π* transition of aromatic C=C bonds, probably influenced by the presence of N functionalizing groups. The band centered at 335 nm corresponds to the first excitonic, also possibly to the n-π* transitions of the C=N bonds. The dispersions of N-GQDs-Ag showed blue-violet emission with a maximum at 441 nm under excitation at 349 nm (Fig. 4 A), with quantum yield (φ) of N-GQDs-Ag estimated at 25% (at 25 °C), using quinine sulfate (347/448 nm) as the comparative standard. The photoluminescence of the N-GQDs-Ag was compared to the one of N-GQDs not modified with silver (refluxed not using Tollen's reagent). Although the intensity of the one modified with silver (right after preparation) is four times less intense, it is more stable, taking about 60 days to decrease to half the initial intensity, while only around 20 days was enough to observe an equivalent photoluminescence decrease from the nanomaterial not modified with Ag.

A study was carried out to obtain a stable and intense signal from the N-GQDs-Ag aqueous dispersion that would allow a reliable analytical response. The stability of the N-GQDs-Ag photoluminescence was studied by monitoring the steady emission intensity from the dispersion, placed on a cuvette every 5 min over a 60 min time interval. Within this time, luminescence remained stable (arbitrary variation of about 2%). The influence of temperature on the photoluminescence from the N-GQDs-Ag dispersion was studied, and a linear and inversely proportional relationship (R2 = 0.990) was found within the tested interval (20 °C–40 °C) with a decrease of approximately 35% of signal intensity as the temperature reached 40 °C. As the temperature was reversed to 20 °C, photoluminescence was re-established to its original intensity (Fig. 4B) with no spectral band shift observed.

The effect of pH was evaluated, taking as a reference the photoluminescence intensity measured at pH 6.7 (the original N-GQDs-Ag after 1000 times dilution with water). The pH of the dispersion varied between 3.0 and 10.0 using aliquots of either HNO3 or NaOH (at 1 × 10−2 mol L−1). It was observed that the greater the acidity of the N-GQDs-Ag dispersion, the greater the suppression of photoluminescence (larger normalized I0/I values) within the pH range between 3.0 and 4.0 (Fig. 4C). However, within the pH range between 6.0 and 9.0, photoluminescence intensity remained constant and no spectral shift was observed. These results are in agreement with the ones from the ζ-potential study, showing that at lower values of pH (3–5), the N atoms are protonated due to the larger concentration of H+ present in the medium, which prevents the transfer of lone pairs of electrons to the graphene core structure, causing the decrease of excitonic recombination and, therefore, the luminescent intensity of N-GQDs-Ag. In contrast, at higher pH values (between 6 and 10), due to the lack of H+ in the medium, the luminescent intensity of N-GQDs-Ag remained stable as there was no competition for the lone pair of electrons from N atoms. Interestingly, even at high pH values, no oxidation of Ag (with the formation of AgOH and ultimately of Ag2O) was observed as the interaction with N-GQDs stabilized silver. Results suggest that as an analytical probe, the N-GQDs-Ag dispersion could be used without any care upon pH adjustment as the photoluminescence was found to be a robust parameter within pH 5.0 and 10.0.

The effect of the continuous exposition of the N-GQDs-Ag dispersion to the excitation radiation (at 349 nm) was evaluated by measuring photoluminescence continuously for 6 min. A small systematic decrease of intensity (under 2.5% or 3.8 arbitrary units min−1) was observed, indicating enough stability to perform photoluminescence measurements as scanning takes less than 1 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: N-GQDs-Ag photoluminescence quenching induced by formaldehyde
Preliminary studies of the interaction between formaldehyde and N-GQDs-Ag
ce:section.ce:para: An aliquot of formaldehyde solution was directly mixed with the luminescent N-GQDs-Ag working dispersion (to obtain a final analyte concentration of 1.5 × 10−5% w/v), and photoluminescence was monitored over time (up to 30 min). Photoluminescence decreased in intensity over the monitored time (Fig. 5 A), and further investigation showed that signal decrease was noticed even after 4 h of the mixing. The test was repeated for different final concentrations of formaldehyde (from 1.5 × 10−4% w/v to 1.5 × 10−1% w/v). The higher the formaldehyde concentration, the faster the photoluminescence quenching since more formaldehyde molecules were available to interact with the nanoparticles in dispersion. While concentrations of 1.5 × 10−4% w/v and 1.5 × 10−3% w/v led to about 50% of the original dispersion signal after 4 h, higher analyte concentrations resulted in a final photoluminescence intensity of about 40% (for 1.5 × 10−2% w/v) and 20% (for 1.5 × 10−1% w/v) of the original dispersion signal, after 3 h, when stabilization was observed.

The test was repeated by probing formaldehyde as vapor in the headspace of a sample vial, using a hanging N-GQDs-Ag dispersion drop (5 μL). The experiment's goal was to concentrate formaldehyde in a micro-volume of dispersion, aiming to achieve a high formaldehyde/nanoparticle ratio that would produce an effective interaction, leading to a fast photoluminescence quenching. Besides providing a concentration of analyte, using a hanging drop is a way to probe the analyte in the gas phase, where most other components (from the sample matrix) would not impose interferences. In this sense, the approach used was a headspace single-drop micro-extraction (HS-SDME). By hanging a single drop over a 1.5 × 10−1% w/v formaldehyde aqueous solution, during periods between 1 and 30 min, before retrieving the drop into the syringe and immediately placing it into the water to measure photoluminescence, an effective quenching (to about 35% of the original dispersion signal) was observed no matter the time exposed to the headspace (Fig. 5B). Similar immediate quenching, reaching a stable signal level, was also observed when the single-drop was hung over more diluted formaldehyde solutions, with quenching varying from 45% (1.5 × 10−2% w/v) and 98% (1.5 × 10−4% w/v) of the original signal.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of temperature upon the interaction of N-GQDs-Ag and formaldehyde
ce:section.ce:para: To understand the mechanism between N-GQDs-Ag and formaldehyde, a study was performed at different temperatures (from 25 °C to 32.5 °C) with increasing concentrations of the analyte. Normalized signal variation, I0/I, was used to present the body of results (where I0 is the dispersion fluorescence measured in the absence of formaldehyde and I was fluorescence measured after interaction with the analyte). First, formaldehyde was added as a solution into the aqueous dispersion to be measured at a fixed temperature (2 min after mixing). Besides the inverse relationship between signal intensity quenching and concentration of formaldehyde, the higher the temperature, the stronger the quenching (higher I0/I value) for a specific concentration of analyte (Fig. 6 A). In addition, a positive deviation of linearity was found due to the more effective quenching at higher concentrations of formaldehyde. This type of polynomial shape indicated a mixture of dynamic and static processes working together to produce overall quenching.

The study was also made using HS-SDME by keeping the sample temperature at different temperatures (25 °C–40 °C) while the drop was exposed to the sample headspace for 5 min before being retrieved into the syringe, then diluted with water to perform the fluorescence measurement at 25 °C. As the temperature increased, fluorescence quenching became more effective (higher I0/I values), reflecting the increase of formaldehyde concentration in the sample headspace (Fig. 6B). A linear relationship was found between normalized signal quenching and concentration of formaldehyde in the sample, showing the importance of controlling temperature during sampling as well as during fluorescence measurements.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical sensing of formaldehyde using N-GQDs-Ag dispersion as a probe
ce:section.ce:para:
The analytical response of the N-GQDs-Ag probe towards formaldehyde, employing HS-SDME, was evaluated using aqueous standards (1 mL) of increasing formaldehyde concentrations (from 1.0 × 10−4% w/v to 1.0 × 10−2% w/v). The sequence of photoluminescence spectra and the normalized analytical curve (Stern-Volmer curve), covering the tested concentration range, is shown in Fig. 7 , along with the residual plot, which shows a random distribution of residues that indicates linear behavior and homoscedasticity of data. The analytical response was linear (R2 = 0.986), covering two orders of magnitude of concentration. The model equation was I
0
/I = (125.5 ± 7.40) [formaldehyde] + (1.00 ± 0.04). The calculated limit of detection (LOD) and the limit of quantification (LOQ) were 1.7 × 10−4% w/v and 5.5 × 10−4% w/v, respectively, calculated based on the concentration of formaldehyde required to decrease the original photoluminescence of the probe (“blank” which is the N-GQDs-Ag dispersion drop not exposed to formaldehyde) equivalent to three times (LOD) and ten times (LOQ) the standard deviation of the “blank” (n = 10). It is important to point out that the method can access this formaldehyde concentration with enough confidence to enable a quantitative measurement. The detection limit was found to be competitive or superior to the ones (converted as %w/v) of other selected methods reported in the literature (Table S1). Also, when compared to other methods [8–11,22–25,34–36] and especially the method recommended in the Brazilian legislation for the determination of formaldehyde in milk [47,48], the method proposed in this work presented advantages because it is simpler than these methods and does not require sample preparation, only a simple dilution with water. Also, the method used in Brazilian legislation is qualitative and used to indicate the presence or absence of formaldehyde (transferred to a chromogenic reactant solution through distillation). In contrast, the proposed method can quantify the amounts of formaldehyde in milk samples.

Reproducibility was estimated after sampling formaldehyde in the drop and diluting it in water, then performing ten consecutive photoluminescence measurements. The test was made using vapor, sampled from formaldehyde solutions at 1.0 × 10−3% w/v and at 1.0 × 10−2% w/v, and the results were respectively 0.6% and 1.8% in terms of signal relative standard deviation. Intermediate precision was obtained by performing vapor sampling in three sets of ten independent formaldehyde solutions (each set containing one different concentration of formaldehyde) and then measuring photoluminescence after diluting the dispersion drop in water. The precision was the standard deviation of the normalized signal ratio (sI0/I), considering the propagation of standard deviations sI0 and sI, which are respectively the ones from the measured set before and after contact with formaldehyde: sI0/I = I0/I × [(sI0/I0)2 + (sI/I)2]1/2. For formaldehyde in aqueous solutions at 1.0 × 10−3% w/v and 1.0 × 10−2% w/v results were 0.7% and 2.0%, respectively.

Hydrogen peroxide is another common milk adulterant; therefore, the effect of H2O2 in the sample was evaluated. First, milk samples were fortified with H2O2 (at concentrations of 2.0 × 10−3% w/v, 2.0 × 10−2% w/v and 2.0 × 10−1% w/v) before using the HS-SDME procedure. It was observed no interference as the probe signal was not affected. The test was repeated in the presence of formaldehyde (at 1.0 × 10−3% w/v and 1.0 × 10−2% w/v) and no interference was observed when the concentration of H2O2 was 2.0 × 10−3% w/v, even at the lower concentration of formaldehyde, not affecting the signal measured after diluting the N-GQDs-Ag probe in water. However, when higher concentrations of H2O2 (2.0 × 10−2% w/v and 2.0 × 10−1% w/v) were present, there was an interaction with formaldehyde in the solution, probably forming formic acid [49], decreasing analyte concentration as vapor, reflected in the interaction with the N-GQDs-Ag probe (quenching less effective than the one expected in the absence of H2O2). In this case, the probe can still be used to indicate that milk is adulterated with formaldehyde.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of the method in milk samples
ce:section.ce:para: The method was tested by determining formaldehyde in different types of milk samples (whole, semi-skimmed and skimmed). A simple dilution with water was used for whole milk (2-fold) and for semi-skimmed milk (1-fold), while no dilution was required for skimmed milk. As formaldehyde was not detected in preliminary measurements, samples were fortified with micro-volumes of a previously titrated formalin solution to achieve final concentrations of 1.0 × 10−4% w/v, 1.0 × 10−3% w/v and 1.0 × 10−2% w/v.

Aliquots of 1.00 mL of the samples were placed inside the glass vial where the syringe needle was inserted, through the rubber septum, to perform the HS-SDME procedure with the hanging N-GQDs-Ag dispersion drop. Quantification was made by interpolation of the experimental results into the analytical curve, previously constructed using aqueous formaldehyde standards submitted to the HS-SDME procedure before re-dissolution of the N-GQDs-Ag probe dispersion for photoluminescence measurements (Table 1 ).

Based on the recovered results (percent recoveries within 93 and 106%), the method was found to be efficient in determining formaldehyde content at 1.0 × 10−3% w/v and at 1.0 × 10−2% w/v (after dilution to adjust concentration in the 10−3% w/v range). Further dilution of samples containing 1.0 × 10−2% w/v of formaldehyde was necessary as recoveries were affected when original solutions were analyzed, leading to percent recoveries between 59% and 75% (depending on the type of milk). There are three possible reasons for that interference, perhaps in association. First, as recoveries were relatively better (about 75%) for samples with less fat (skimmed milk), as it may be hindering volatilization of formaldehyde, not leading to a proportional quantity in the form of vapor in the headspace of the sample vial, another possibility is that the interaction between vapor and the hanging dispersion drop is affected when higher formaldehyde concentrations are present in the sample vial headspace. Finally, the lower recoveries may be caused during the photoluminescence measurement after dilution of the drop with water due to the instability of photoluminescence of quantum dots when higher concentrations of formaldehyde are present.

The method's selectivity was confirmed by testing the effect of different volatile organic compounds (VOCs) that may be present in milk. Milk samples were fortified with high concentrations (1.0% w/v) of different VOCs before being submitted to the proposed method based on the HS-SDME technique and photoluminescence measurements from N-GQDs-Ag. Results showed (Fig. S5) that selected VOCs from the classes of ketone, alcohol, ethyl ester, nitrogen-containing compounds and dimethyl sulfone did not affect the photoluminescence of N-GQDs-Ag as signal quenching was not significant (variation around only 1%). When the test was made with high concentrations of different aldehydes (1.0% w/v), photoluminescence quenching of the N-GQDs-Ag dispersion of about 20% (for benzaldehyde) and 2% (for trimethylacetaldehyde) was observed. At the concentration of 1.0 × 10−2% w/v, benzaldehyde produced a quenching of about 6%, while trimethylacetaldehyde did not make any quenching. No signal quenching was observed when benzaldehyde concentration was reduced to 1.0 × 10−3% w/v. These results indicated the method's selectivity towards the volatile formaldehyde, considering that other aldehydes (less volatile ones) require very high concentrations in milk to produce quenching.

To compare the method's performance, the formaldehyde content was determined in whole milk samples using the HPLC method based on the absorption photometric measurement after liquid-liquid extraction and chemical derivatization of formaldehyde with DNPH solution. Formaldehyde fortification was made at two levels in authentic replicates (1.0 × 10−3% w/v and 1.0 × 10−2% w/v). A typical DNPH-formaldehyde chromatogram is shown in Fig. S6 with analytical curve equation of Y = (3.23 ± 0.2) × 105 [HCOH] + (9.4 ± 11.8) with satisfactory linear response (R2 = 0.987). The result obtained for the samples fortified at 1.0 × 10−3% w/v was (9.9 ± 0.2) × 10−4% w/v (99% recovery) while (1.1 ± 0.03) × 10−2% w/v (110% recovery) was achieved for the sample fortified at 1.0 × 10−2% w/v. Recovery results obtained using the proposed method were compared with the ones obtained using the HPLC method (Table 2 ).

Taking into consideration the results obtained for the whole milk samples, the proposed method and the chromatographic method were statistically equal (two-tail Student's t-test, with 95% confidence limit at n = 3) in terms of quantitative performance, considering the sample fortified at 1.0 × 10−3% w/v (tcalculated = 3.415) and at 1.0 × 10−2% w/v after dilution (tcalculated = 3.705) and considering the critical statistical value (tcritical = 2.776).

An analytical probe based on photoluminescent nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots modified with silver (N-GQDs-Ag) was used successfully to quantify formaldehyde in milk. N-GQDs-Ag were prepared by a hydrothermal refluxing route, enabling enough photoluminescence stability and prompt interaction with formaldehyde that produced quenching that are simultaneously static and dynamic in nature play. Characterization of the nanomaterial indicated the presence of nitrogen-containing functional groups as substituents onto the carbon nanomaterial and possible decoration with Ag(NH)2 +.

Formaldehyde was determined by probing the headspace of the sample vial using the HS-SDME technique. This approach minimized interferences from matrix components of milk, as probing was made in the vapor phase, allowing identification of the adulterant at concentrations below 1.0 × 10−3% w/v. The method is simple as it does not require sample preparation (besides a simple dilution), and results were compared to the ones obtained by HPLC after liquid-liquid extraction and analyte chemical derivatization. In this way, the proposed method can readily provide information to guarantee the safety of milk consumers.
Nothing to declare.
